content:
CrashLoopBackOff isn’t the problem — it’s the symptom. Learn the debugging workflow that reveals why your Pod keeps restarting.

13 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

You deploy your checkout service. For about ten seconds, everything looks fine. Then you run the command everyone runs first:
kubectl get podsNAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE
checkout-api-7d9c-x2k 0/1 CrashLoopBackOff 5 4m
CrashLoopBackOff. The RESTARTS counter is climbing. READY says 0/1. Your app never becomes available.
So you delete the pod. Kubernetes makes a new one. Same thing. You bump the replica count from 2 to 4, hoping one of them survives. Now you have four pods in CrashLoopBackOff instead of two. You've made it worse.
Here’s the part almost nobody tells you on day one: CrashLoopBackOff is not an error. It's not even a problem, exactly. It's Kubernetes reporting on its own behaviour. And once you understand what it's actually saying, the fix stops being "restart it again" and becomes "find the one thing that made the container exit."
